cataloging_subject.label: The Epistle of Othea
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Stephen Scrope's Middle English translation of Christine de Pizan's Epistre Othea, made around 1440, including Scrope's prose dedication to Sir John Fastolf and the one-hundred-chapter texte, glose, and allegorie design. CME00015 represents the incomplete Longleat House MS 253 form through Warner's 1904 edition. The cataloged work is not Christine's French source work, any physical manuscript, Warner's printed edition, Schieberle's modern edition, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML.

catalog_summary.title: The Epistle of Othea
catalog_summary.author: Christine de Pizan; translated by Stephen Scrope
catalog_summary.date: Translated around 1440; first printed from Longleat House MS 253 in 1904
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of translation unknown

assertion.notes: The short form is used by modern scholarship for Scrope's translation and avoids treating Warner's two-part title as the only work title.

titles.value: The Epistle of Othea to Hector

notes: The alternate title printed after OR on Warner's title page and preserved in the CME encoding.

agents.name: Christine de Pizan
notes: Author of the French Epistre Othea translated by Scrope; the catalog record does not collapse the French source work into the Middle English translation.

agents.name: Stephen Scrope
notes: Translator of the Middle English Epistle around 1440 and author of its Fastolf dedication.

agents.name: George F. Warner
notes: Editor and transcriber of the 1904 Longleat-based source edition represented by CME00015.

scope.description: Authorship of the French work translated throughout Scrope's Middle English version

assertion.notes: This source authorship is distinct from responsibility for Scrope's English wording and Fastolf dedication.

scope.description: The 1904 edition used by the U-M encoding
Longleat House MS 253, fols. 2r-75v, as represented on printed pp. 1-114

assertion.notes: The date belongs to Scrope's act of translation, not automatically to any extant witness.

date_statements.display: First printed in 1904 in George F. Warner's edition

assertion.notes: Warner states that he prints the English text for the first time.
Scrope's English translation and Fastolf dedication support an English cultural origin, but the inspected evidence does not establish an exact place of translation.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Warner supplies some Latin authorities in brackets from comparison material where Longleat left spaces; those editorial supplies are not treated as a second intrinsic language of the Longleat-form work record.

form_statements.description: A prose Fastolf dedication followed by chapters that combine a verse texte with prose glose and prose allegorie.

subject_statements.label: Classical mythology interpreted through Christian moral theology

summaries.value: In a prose dedication, Stephen Scrope offers Sir John Fastolf his Middle English translation of Christine de Pizan's Epistre Othea. Othea, figured as a goddess of prudence, instructs the young Hector through one hundred concise mythological exempla. Each chapter normally joins a verse texte to prose moral explanation and Christian allegory, turning stories of classical gods, heroes, and the Trojan War into counsel on chivalry, governance, virtues and vices, spiritual conduct, and the authority of learned women.

The conceptual work has one hundred chapters, but the Longleat witness represented by Warner and CME00015 is materially incomplete: it lacks a leaf after fol. 9v and a quire after fol. 34v. The encoding contains 89 numbered chapters, the surviving tail of chapter 49 as an unnumbered division, and an explicit lacuna; it must not be used to infer a ninety-chapter original.

extent.description: Conceptually, one prose dedication and one hundred mixed-form chapters. The incomplete Longleat-derived XML represents 89 numbered chapter DIV2 elements, one unnumbered surviving chapter-49 fragment, one lacuna placeholder, 90 commentary DIV3 elements, 88 allegorical-interpretation DIV3 elements, 481 verse L elements outside notes, 188 BODY P elements, 492 BODY NOTE elements, 115 PB elements, and 72 MILESTONE elements. The 481-line count is an encoding metric for surviving represented verse, not the complete work's canonical verse extent.

content_parts.label: Scrope's dedication to Sir John Fastolf
content_parts.summary: A prose dedicatory preface identifying Scrope's translation and presenting it to his stepfather, Sir John Fastolf.
notes: The approximate Longleat subrange is inferred from the edition and manuscript milestones; the securely established complete work locus is ff. 2r-75v.

content_parts.label: One hundred Othea chapters
content_parts.summary: The designed sequence of one hundred mythological instruction units, each normally comprising a verse texte, a prose glose, and a prose allegorie.

locators.value: approximately ff. 5r-75v, with physical losses after ff. 9v and 34v

notes: The item count describes the work's designed extent; only 90 chapter units are represented at least in part by the incomplete Longleat-derived text.

related_works.title: Epistre Othea
notes: Christine composed the French source work around 1400. No evidence supports identifying Bodleian Laud Misc. 570 or Harley MS 4431 as Scrope's direct physical exemplar.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00015.xml.
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for identifiers, title-page transcription, Middle English language, Scrope/Fastolf attribution, Warner edition boundary, surviving text, notes, divisions, page breaks, folio milestones, lacunae, and the 15 June 2016 structural review.

evidence.citation: Christine de Pizan, The Epistle of Othea to Hector or The Boke of Knyghthode, translated from the French by Stephen Scrope, edited from a manuscript in the library of the Marquis of Bath by George F. Warner (London: J. B. Nichols and Sons, 1904).
evidence.locator: Title page; introduction pp. i-xlvii; English text pp. 1-114; glossary and index
evidence.summary: Immediate source-edition evidence for title, Christine and Scrope responsibilities, Fastolf dedication, Longleat source, approximate translation date, manuscript losses, chapter structure, editorial comparison with Harley MS 4431, and first publication of the English text.

evidence.citation: Christine de Pizan, Advice for Princes in Middle English Translation: Stephen Scrope's The Epistle of Othea and the Anonymous Lytle Bibell of Knyghthod, edited by Misty Schieberle (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2020).
evidence.locator: Introduction; The Epistle of Othea, printed pp. 33-134
evidence.summary: Current scholarly evidence for translation date, three English witnesses and their relations, the S-base/M-and-L-collated edition, Longleat losses, French-source family, genre, form, chapter design, themes, and the warning that the Fastolf-associated Laud manuscript is not a demonstrated direct exemplar.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00015.
evidence.locator: Item identity, publication statement, and current rights badge
evidence.summary: Responsible digital-collection evidence for the stable CME identifier, Warner 1904 publication metadata, and current No Copyright-United States item statement.

evidence.locator: Counts of DIV1, DIV2, DIV3, P, L, LG, NOTE, PB, and MILESTONE; exclusion of NOTE ancestors for work verse
evidence.summary: Reproducibly identifies 3 DIV1 containers, 89 numbered chapter DIV2 elements, one unnumbered chapter-49 fragment, one lacuna placeholder, 90 commentary and 88 allegorical DIV3 elements, 481 non-note verse lines, 188 BODY paragraphs, 492 BODY notes, 115 page breaks, and 72 milestones. These are surviving encoding metrics, not a revision of the work's one-hundred-chapter design.

open_questions.question: Can Scrope's lost French exemplar be identified more precisely within the BI manuscript family?
next_steps: Compare Scrope's readings against the full BI-family apparatus in Schieberle and specialist French editions.
Keep Bodleian Laud Misc. 570 as a sister manuscript associated with Fastolf, not a direct-exemplar claim.
Do not infer physical descent from textual closeness alone.

open_questions.question: Where exactly did Scrope make the translation, and for what initial production context?
next_steps: Consult documentary studies of Scrope and Fastolf's household around 1440.
Distinguish the translation's date and place from the later production of each extant witness.
Retain England as cultural origin and leave exact place unknown until directly supported.

open_questions.question: Can the exact Longleat folio boundary between Scrope's Fastolf preface and chapter 1 be verified from complete manuscript images?
next_steps: Request complete photography of Longleat House MS 253, including binding, endleaves, blanks, all 95 folios, and the adjacent Tree of Love.
Map the Epistle securely to ff. 2r-75v and then refine internal preface/chapter boundaries from the images.
Confirm retention and reuse terms before caching any supplied files.

notes: The work record describes Scrope's translation, while the lineage manifest models the three English witnesses, two French comparison witnesses, editions, digital encoding, repository artifact, and image surrogates separately.
Longleat's material losses are not converted into a shorter conceptual work extent.
Warner's supplied Latin readings and French comparison notes belong to the edition layer and are not silently attributed to the incomplete Longleat manuscript.
The adjacent Tree of Love in Longleat MS 253, Warner's introduction/glossary/index, and Schieberle's separately edited Lytle Bibell of Knyghthod are outside the cataloging boundary.
